explicit_text: When President John F. Kennedy was shot, everyone knew exactly where they were, and it was a moment that stuck with me. I was reminded of that day when the September 11 attacks happened because of the immediate shock and urgency. The comparison highlights how significant and unforgettable those moments were for the nation and for those of us in law enforcement.

explicit_text: During the September 11 attacks, I was in a multi-agency meeting that included the United States Attorney. explicit_text: On September 11, I was at a multi-agency meeting when the first tower was hit, and the situation rapidly escalated with the second tower and the Pentagon being attacked. We immediately began evacuating the monuments and memorials in Washington, D.C., and secured key areas like the White House and President's Park. The Park Police worked around the clock, calling in off-duty officers and managing chaotic evacuations and road closures to protect the city and its officials.

explicit_text: On September 11, the Memorial Bridge was initially closed by the Park Police due to fears of another attack, possibly a plane crash or explosion. This closure, along with the 14th Street Bridge closure, severely limited traffic flow out of the city, causing major evacuation problems. We reopened Memorial Bridge within an hour to relieve traffic congestion and help people evacuate into Virginia.

explicit_text: Rock Creek Parkway played an important role in the evacuation on September 11. This adjustment was part of the traffic management efforts to deal with the gridlock caused by bridge closures and mass evacuations.
